method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The name "Nishinoshima" translates from Japanese as "western island," reflecting its geographic position within the Ogasawara (Bonin) island group, located approximately 1,000 kilometres south of Tokyo.[2]

The island was first recorded by Europeans in 1702, when it was sighted by the Spanish ship Rosario, after which it was initially named "Rosario Island."[3] It was subsequently renamed Nishinoshima, the name by which it is known today.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an andesitic volcanic island located at the summit of a large submarine volcano approximately 30 km in diameter.[1] The island lies approximately 1,000 km south of Tokyo and 130 km west of Chichijima in the Ogasawara island chain, and forms part of the volcanic front (Shichito-Iwojima Ridge) of the Izu-Bonin arc, which trends in a north–south direction parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanic activity in this region is caused by the subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] Nishinoshima has also been classified as a back-arc subduction volcano situated beside a spreading-centre graben known as the Ogasawara Trough.[2]

## Structure

The volcanic edifice consists of a main peak that appears above sea level as an island, together with two submarine peaks located to the west and south of the island.[4] A small cone is present on the northeast flank of the main peak, which may be a satellite cone.[4] Prior to the 2013 eruption, the subaerial island was approximately 650 m long (southwest to northeast) and approximately 200 m wide, with an area of roughly 77,000 m², an elevation of 25 m, and several reefs on its northeast side.[1] North of Nishinoshima, large submarine stratovolcanoes with basal diameters of 20–30 km rise 2,200–2,800 m above their surrounding sea floor.[4]

## Petrology and magma composition

The SiO₂ content of ejecta from Nishinoshima ranges between 51.8 and 60.1 wt%.[1] Analysis of submarine deposits has revealed that the 2020 eruption of Nishinoshima consisted of basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas, with the andesites being similar to those which had erupted from the island since 1973.[3] Nishinoshima andesite primary magmas originate directly from the mantle as a result of shallow and hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3] The 2020 basalts are comparable to older basalts previously erupted in the surrounding knolls, suggesting that basalt magmas may have been generated throughout the entire history of Nishinoshima, with their eruption center having moved to the island summit in 2020.[3]

## Eruptive history

The first eruption in recorded history occurred in May 1973 on the sea floor approximately 400 m east of the southern tip of the island, forming a new island by September of that year.[1] The eruption began as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion and moved semicircularly, with five craters ejecting volcanic blocks and lava flow.[1] Each volcanic edifice lost the majority of its structure due to severe erosion, but removed deposits linked the old and new island, and the mouth of the bay became closed off by deposit movement in 1982.[1] On December 21, 1973, the Maritime Safety Agency named the new land form Nishinoshima Shinto.[1]

In November and December of 2013, a new eruption produced a new island, which subsequently merged with the older island.[2] After the first eruption ended in November 2015, scientists landed on the island to observe how life would return, believing that the volcano would remain dormant for some time.[2] However, the volcano returned to a state of eruption in April 2017, with activity lasting into August 2018.[2]

The 2013–2020 eruptive activity can be divided into four episodes.[3] The duration of the eruptive episodes was initially 2 years (Episode 1) and 120 days (Episode 2), but shortened to a week by Episode 3, with decreasing eruptive volume over time; Episode 4, in 2019–2020, lasted 270 days.[3] Average effusion rates for the 2013–2015 eruption (Episode 1) and the 2017 eruption (Episode 2) were estimated at 2.0 × 10⁵ m³/day and 1.5 × 10⁵ m³/day, respectively.[3] Episode 4 was characterized first by Strombolian activity with lava effusion, then lava-fountain eruptions, and finally ash emission likely generated by phreatomagmatic eruptions, representing a style of activity that had never previously occurred at Nishinoshima.[4]

## Monitoring

Throughout the four episodes of the 2013–2020 Nishinoshima eruption, remote observations from satellites, airplanes, and research vessels were used to monitor the volcano.[4] In addition to optical satellite imagery, synthetic aperture radar satellite data (TerraSAR-X, ALOS-2) were used to constrain temporal changes in the eruption processes, as SAR observations are not affected by clouds or eruption plumes due to their use of long-wavelength electromagnetic waves.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima offers researchers a rare, real-time case study of island colonization and ecosystem development following volcanic emergence, making it a natural laboratory for observing primary succession on an isolated oceanic island.[3] As part of the wider Ogasawara Archipelago, it lies within a region characterized by peculiar island ecosystems shaped by unique processes of adaptive radiation and speciation, with biota originating from diverse sources including Oceania, Southeast Asia, and the Japanese main islands.[5]

Because the island is frequently reshaped by volcanic eruptions and marine erosion, much of its plant life has been repeatedly eliminated and must recolonize from scratch.[5] Early botanical surveys following the 1973–1974 eruptions documented the invasion of pioneer plants onto the newly formed volcanic surfaces of Nishinoshima Shinto.[4] Among the species that established itself on the island was common purslane (Portulaca oleracea), a cosmopolitan plant found across tropical regions worldwide.[2]

Prior to a major eruption in 2019 that devastated the island's vegetation, researchers from Tokyo Metropolitan University collected specimens of the native Nishinoshima purslane and transported them to their laboratory for cultivation and study.[2] The subsequent volcanic activity is reported to have driven the island's purslane population to extinction in situ, illustrating how volcanic processes can directly cause the loss of local plant populations even as new land is created.[2]

Coral reef development around Nishinoshima is also being monitored to understand how marine biodiversity becomes established in newly formed coastal environments, complementing terrestrial studies of species colonization.[3] Ongoing research partnerships between Japanese institutions and international organizations continue to document the evolutionary processes and ecosystem resilience displayed by the island.[3]
